basic_info.name: Food Packaging Migration Risk
basic_info.name_ja: 食品包装マイグレーションリスク
basic_info.description: The RED (Relative Energy Difference) between food-contact packaging polymers and food components (simulants, monomers, additives) predicts the migration risk of potentially harmful substances from packaging into food. Low RED values indicate high affinity between the polymer and the migrant, meaning the substance is more soluble in the polymer and more likely to migrate. This HSP-based approach supports regulatory screening under EFSA and FDA frameworks for food contact materials, enabling rapid assessment of worst-case migration scenarios before costly experimental testing.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Migration of substances from packaging polymer into food is governed by thermodynamic partitioning and diffusion kinetics. The HSP distance between a migrant (monomer, additive, plasticizer, or degradation product) and the food simulant determines the thermodynamic driving force for migration. When RED between the migrant and the food simulant is low (high affinity), the migrant partitions favorably into the food phase. Simultaneously, the migrant's solubility in the polymer matrix (governed by RED between migrant and polymer) determines its availability for diffusion. A migrant that is highly soluble in the polymer (low RED with polymer) but also highly compatible with the food (low RED with food simulant) presents maximum migration risk. The approach extends to predicting which food simulant (aqueous, acidic, alcoholic, fatty) extracts the most from a given polymer, guiding worst-case testing strategies.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_migrant-dD_simulant)^2 + (dP_migrant-dP_simulant)^2 + (dH_migrant-dH_simulant)^2); RED_migrant-food = Ra/R0; Partition coefficient K ~ exp(-Ra^2*V/(RT)); Migration M ~ K * D * sqrt(t)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED(migrant-food) < 1.0 indicates high migration risk into that food type; RED(migrant-polymer) < 1.0 indicates migrant is soluble in polymer and available for transport

required_inputs: Packaging polymer HSP (dD, dP, dH) and interaction radius R0
Migrant substance HSP (dD, dP, dH) - for monomers, additives, NIAS
Food simulant HSP (dD, dP, dH) - water, 3% acetic acid, 10-50% ethanol, vegetable oil
Migration limit (SML or OML from regulation)
data_sources: EU JRC (Joint Research Centre) database for food contact substances
FDA Chemistry data for Indirect Food Additives
Group contribution estimation for novel additives and NIAS
Published polymer HSP compilations (Van Krevelen, Barton)
EFSA scientific opinions on specific substances
complementary_parameters: Diffusion coefficient of migrant in polymer (Dp)
Migrant molecular weight and molecular volume
Surface area to volume ratio of packaging
Multilayer structure and barrier layer properties

limitations: HSP alone predicts thermodynamic compatibility but not diffusion kinetics, which depend on polymer free volume, crystallinity, and migrant molecular size
Non-intentionally added substances (NIAS) such as degradation products or reaction byproducts often have unknown HSP
Polymer crystallinity significantly reduces migration but is not captured in amorphous-phase HSP values
Food matrices are far more complex than simple simulants; real food interactions may deviate from simulant predictions
Multilayer packaging requires modeling of lag time through barrier layers
Set-off migration (from outer printing inks through substrate to food) involves different mechanisms
known_failure_modes: Highly crystalline polymers (HDPE, PET) where migration is much lower than predicted from amorphous HSP due to crystalline barrier
Low-molecular-weight migrants that diffuse rapidly regardless of HSP compatibility due to small molecular size
Reactive migrants (e.g., isocyanates, epoxides) that transform chemically during migration
Nanocomposite packaging where nanoparticle-polymer interactions modify effective polymer HSP and barrier properties
validation_method: Migration testing per EU 10/2011 (total and specific migration)
GC-MS or LC-MS for identification and quantification of migrants in food simulants
Mathematical modeling using Piringer/Begley diffusion models with HSP-derived partition coefficients
Accelerated migration testing at elevated temperature (e.g., 60C/10 days for long-term room temperature)
Cell-based bioassays for overall migrate toxicity screening

key_references.author: Piringer, O.G.; Baner, A.L.
key_references.title: Plastic Packaging: Interactions with Food and Pharmaceuticals, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Gillet, G.; Vitrac, O.; Desobry, S.
key_references.title: Prediction of partition coefficients of plastic additives between packaging materials and food simulants

key_references.author: Begley, T.; Castle, L.; Feigenbaum, A.; et al.
key_references.title: Evaluation of migration models that might be used in support of regulations for food-contact plastics

key_references.author: Dole, P.; Feigenbaum, A.E.; De la Cruz, C.; et al.
key_references.title: Typical diffusion behaviour in packaging polymers - application to functional barriers

literature.regulatory_relevance: Directly relevant to EFSA food contact material evaluation (EU Regulation 10/2011) and FDA food contact notification (FCN) process. HSP-based partition coefficient estimation is used in mathematical migration modeling accepted by EU JRC and FDA as supportive evidence. EFSA guidelines recognize modeled migration data as a first screening tier before experimental testing. EU Framework Regulation (EC) No 1935/2004 requires demonstration of safety for all food contact materials.
